region_of_origin: United States (Tex-Mex tradition); adapted in Singapore

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for vegetarian fajitas; A0CKP (composite vegetable dish in flatbread) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for vegetarian fajitas as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of vegetarian fajitas consists of two filled tortillas weighing approximately 250 g, containing grilled vegetables, beans, cheese, and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetarian Fajitas — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Vegetarian Fajitas (cr_id G00010820). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite vegetable fajita profile (USDA SR Legacy #21340) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; all other values are HPB-sourced.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable and bean wrap with flour tortilla
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for vegetarian fajitas. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for a mixed bean and vegetable wrap in a flour tortilla (GI ~40–50, low category). High fibre content (6.1 g/100g) and low carbohydrate (9.0 g/100g) support a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (45 × 9.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 10.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 490 mg/100g is borderline high, partially offset by potassium 600 mg/100g (K:Na ratio >1.2)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request low-sodium fajita seasoning and limit portion to one tortilla to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal; the high potassium content (600 mg/100g) provides a favourable DASH-diet offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (9.0 g/100g), high dietary fibre (6.1 g/100g), and high protein (23.0 g/100g) produce a low glycaemic load and attenuate postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Vegetarian fajitas are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when served with whole-wheat tortillas; the estimated GI of 45 and GL of ~10 per serving support stable postprandial blood glucose, and the high protein and fibre content further slow gastric emptying.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol-free (0 mg/100g), high fibre (6.1 g/100g), and moderate saturated fat (~3.5 g/100g) with plant-based protein profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vegetarian fajitas are a heart-friendly option due to zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre supporting LDL reduction, and a plant-based protein base; patients should choose avocado-based guacamole over sour cream to further reduce saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High fibre supports gut microbiome and immune regulation; bell peppers provide vitamin C and antioxidants; nightshade vegetables may trigger flares in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to anti-inflammatory fibre and antioxidant content; however, individuals sensitive to nightshades (bell peppers, tomatoes, chilli) should monitor for joint flare responses and consider substituting with non-nightshade vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Fajitas are a Tex-Mex-inspired dish consisting of grilled or stir-fried vegetables — typically bell peppers, onions, and mushrooms — combined with black beans and wrapped in a flour tortilla, served with condiments such as guacamole, sour cream, and salsa. The dish is characterised by a savoury, mildly spiced flavour profile with a soft tortilla exterior and a tender, slightly charred vegetable filling. In Singapore, vegetarian fajitas are commonly served as a lunch or dinner option at Western casual dining restaurants. Nutritionally, the dish is notable for its high protein content (23.0 g/100g, likely contributed by black beans and cheese), high dietary fibre (6.1 g/100g), and low carbohydrate content (9.0 g/100g), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The potassium-to-sodium ratio exceeds 1.2, supporting cardiovascular and blood pressure health. Cholesterol is zero, and the plant-based protein profile makes it appropriate for vegetarians. The estimated glycaemic index of 45 places it in the low-GI category, and the glycaemic load per standard serving (~250 g) is approximately 10, which is clinically favourable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-friendly and can be made vegan by omitting dairy-based condiments.
common_user_queries: How many calories in vegetarian fajitas?
Are vegetarian fajitas good for diabetics?
Is vegetarian fajita high in sodium?
Can I eat vegetarian fajitas if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of vegetarian fajitas?
Are vegetarian fajitas suitable for weight loss?
What vegetables are in vegetarian fajitas?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010820, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite vegetable fajita profile as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for a comparable bean-and-vegetable wrap.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (MUFA, PUFA, trans fat) for this composite vegetarian fajita preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database (G00010820) nor in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #21340 for a comparable composite vegetable fajita as of 2026; no approved source provides these values for this dish. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1: individual vitamin micronutrient data for this composite vegetarian fajita are not reported in the HPB Foodo database (G00010820) or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026; no approved source provides per-100g vitamin breakdown for this specific dish preparation. phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these mineral micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database (G00010820) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for a comparable composite vegetable fajita as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite vegetarian fajita preparation is not reported in the HPB Foodo database (G00010820) or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source (G00010820); consistent with a plant-based dish containing no meat or egg yolk. Saturated fat of 3.5 g/100g is estimated from USDA SR Legacy entry for a comparable composite vegetable fajita (closest approved-source analogue); documented as an estimate. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed bean and vegetable wraps in flour tortillas. This dish is classified as Western (Tex-Mex) cuisine adapted for the Singapore market; multilingual names provided for Singaporean context per G6 rules. food_subgroup updated from 'Batter-based dishes' to 'Wraps and filled flatbreads' as fajitas are tortilla-wrapped dishes, not batter-based.
